title: Nuno felting on silk organza
subtitle: Merino roving felted into lightweight silk organza to produce a sheer, textured cloth
excerpt: Nuno felt a 60 x 60 cm scarf or panel by laying fine merino roving directly onto silk organza and wet-felting the two together. As the merino shrinks and locks into the open weave of the organza, the silk gathers and scrunches into a pleated, textured surface. The finished cloth is lightweight, drapy, and machine-hand-washable at 30 degrees.
sourceNotes: Nuno felting on silk organza is a contemporary textile technique developed in the late 20th century from the Japanese-derived nuno tradition. Method draws on workshop practice in the UK and Australia.

glossaryTerms.definition: The process in nuno felting where wet felting causes wool fibres to push through the open weave of a woven fabric and lock on the other side. The migration is what bonds the roving to the base fabric permanently.

content.text: Organza has a tighter, stiffer weave than chiffon, which means the wool fibres must work harder to migrate through it. The result is a cloth with more defined texture: the silk crinkles into sharp pleats rather than soft gathers, and the finished surface has a sculptural quality. Use fine merino at 18-21 micron and lay the roving sparingly to preserve the sheerness of the organza.

items.name: Felting net or light cotton voile
Pool noodle or foam roller for rolling

content.text: Lay the organza flat on the bamboo mat. Pull fine wisps of merino and lay them in one direction across the organza surface, covering perhaps 60-70% of the silk rather than the full surface. A full cover produces a dense opaque cloth; partial coverage keeps the organza visible through gaps in the wool. Lay a second, sparse layer at 90 degrees to the first. The combined layers should be visibly thin enough to see the silk beneath.

Cover with the felting net. Pour hot soapy water slowly over the net from a height. The water must reach the silk and wet it fully, but the pour must be gentle enough not to shift the roving before the net holds it in place. Press firmly through the net until the silk is saturated and the roving begins to cling.
Rub gently through the net in circular strokes for 10 minutes. The roving must begin to lock through the organza weave: this is
. After 10 minutes, lift a corner of the net and pinch the organza and roving together. If the roving peels off easily, continue rubbing. Once the roving holds on its own, remove the net.

Roll the piece around the pool noodle (without the net), wrap in the bamboo mat, and roll 40-50 passes. Unroll, turn 90 degrees, and repeat. The silk will begin to scrunch and the piece will shrink noticeably. Continue rolling in alternating directions until the silk is visibly gathered and the wool surface is firm.

Rinse in warm then cold water. Squeeze gently (never wring). Allow to dry flat or draped over a rail; the silk can take the weight of the piece in a way that heavy pure wool cannot. The finished cloth can be hand-washed at 30 degrees.

items.problem: The roving lifts off the organza in sheets rather than locking in
items.cause: Organza not wet enough, or fibre migration not complete before rolling
items.fix: Spend more time in the initial flat-rubbing phase. Lift and test frequently. Do not begin rolling until the roving is firmly attached.

items.problem: The finished cloth is stiff and board-like rather than drapy
items.fix: In the next piece, reduce the roving by about a third. Sheer, drapy nuno relies on very little wool relative to the silk area.

items.problem: The organza tears at the pleating points during fulling
items.cause: Organza quality too low or fibres migrated too aggressively
items.fix: Use a heavier-weight organza (at least 60 g per square metre) and work with slightly cooler water to slow the migration rate.
